notes: The battle described here of Gog and Magog against Israel will be "in the latter days" (vs. 8, 16) after the people of Israel have gathered back to their land and "dwelleth safely" (v. 14). In the Doctrine and Covenants the Lord reaffirms that the destructions "spoken by the mouth of Ezekiel the prophet" (29:21) will occur "before this great day [the second coming of Christ in power and great glory] shall come." (D&C 29:14.) A second battle of Gog and Magog against the covenant people of the Lord will occur after the Millennium, according to the Lord as revealed to John the Apostle. (Rev. 20:8.) Daniel H. Ludlow, A Companion to Your Study of the Old Testament, 344.)

quotes.text: After the gathering but before the kingdom of peace shall be established, one last mighty attack will be mounted against the Lord's potential kingdom. The attackers will come from all sides. Gog, "chief prince of Meshech and Tubal," is evidently of the Japhetic peoples of the Caucasus and northward, as are Gomer and the house of Togarmah (Gen. 10:2; BD, "Gog"). Persia comes from the east, Ethiopia the south, and Libya the west; thus, they may represent all nations (Ezek. 38:2-7; Zech. 14:1-3).
quotes.speaker: Ellis T. Rasmussen
quotes.source: A Latter-day Saint Commentary on the Old Testament
quotes.attr: Ellis T. Rasmussen, A Latter-day Saint Commentary on the Old Testament, 609

notes: The Gog and Magog prophecy of Ezek. 38-39 is paralleled by John's vision in Rev. 17-20, part of which speaks about the great "whore," the "mother of harlots" (Rev. 17:1-6, 15-18) which falls (Rev. 18:1-3) and whose "smoke rose up for ever and ever" (Rev. 19:1-3). John wrote of the war of Gog and Magog, wherein the forces of evil are devoured by fire from heaven and the devil who deceived them is "cast into the lake of fire and brimstone" with his followers (Rev. 19:20; 20:7, 14; 21:8). The fire from heaven that destroys the army of Gog and Magog is also mentioned in Ezek. 39:6, while fire and brimstone are in Ezek. 38:22.

Most of modern Europe and parts of Asia Minor Gomer, another descendant of Japheth (see Genesis 10:2), is thought by most scholars to be the ancestor of the people known by classical historians as the Cimmerians. The Cimmerians were the source of such later peoples as the Theric, Gaelic, Celtic, British, and Teutonic tribes. Togarmah (v. 6)

Gog "mountain" the prince of the land of Magog. The title was probably a common one of the kings of the country, as "Pharaoh" in Egypt. Ezekiel may have used Gog and Magog from the historical fact familiar to men at the time, as ideal titles for the great last anti-Christian confederacy.

The southern part of the continent of Africa In Hebrew Cush usually represented the whole of Africa lying south of Egypt and the north African coastal countries. Libya (v. 5) The northern African countries Libya (Phut in Hebrew) generally meant North Africa except Egypt.

Nephi writes of the "great and abominable church," while Ezek. 38:7 speaks of Gog's assembly ("they that are assembled unto thee"), which is the meaning of the word church. The tie is somewhat lost in the King James translation, which speaks of "thy company that are assembled unto thee." In the Hebrew text, both the verb and the noun derive from the root qhl, which means "to assemble." Significantly, Nephi notes that the "great church" would be formed "among the nations of the Gentiles" (1 Ne. 13:4).

This is probably the prophecy mentioned in D&C 29:21: And the great and aabominable church, which is the bwhore of all the earth, shall be cast down by cdevouring fire, according as it is spoken by the mouth of Ezekiel the prophet, who spoke of these things, which have not come to pass but surely dmust, as I live, for eabominations shall not reign.
cloud ('anan) the same word used in Exodus to represent the presence of the Lord
So in Ex 9:16, God tells Pharaoh, "For this cause have I raised thee up, for to show in thee My power; and that My name may be declared throughout all the earth."
face (Pa 'aph) literally, "nose"; in Hebrew, the idiomatic expression for anger, as men in anger breathe strongly through the nostrils
steep places (madregah) literally, "stairs" (So 2:14); steep terraces for vines on the sides of hills, to prevent the earth being washed down by the rains
The imagery is taken from the destruction of Sodom and the plagues of Egypt.
